entry.title: SAP Build Code新機能：AI Chat
entry.content:
SAP Build Codeに新機能が搭載されましたのでご紹介したいと思います。この機能はbuild-codeプランあるいはbuild-defaultプランのSAP Build Codeで使用可能な機能です。BASでは使用することが出来ません。

SAP Build CodeにAI Chat機能としてチャットによるプログラミング・作業アシスト機能が搭載されました。バイブコーディングが可能になったと言ってもよいでしょう。
https://help.sap.com/docs/build_code/d0d8f5bfc3d640478854e6f4e7c7584a/071e0b759ae4415e94a0dcaaecf99fd7.html?locale=en-US
GitHub Copilotベースの機能です。

この機能を有効化するには、SAP Build LobbyのControl TowerでGenerative AI機能を有効化する必要があります。

これまでにSAP Build CodeやSAP Build Process AutomationでJoule for Developerの機能を使用されていた方は既に有効化されていると思われます。

この新機能を使用することで、いわゆるバイブコーディングが利用可能になります。また、事前定義済みLLMとしていくつかのLLMが利用可能になっています。
機能を呼び出すには

SAP Build Code画面上部に追加された吹き出し型のアイコン、もしくは左側の三本線のメニューからView→Chatで右側にチャットウインドウが開きます。

この機能ですが、事前定義済みLLMとしていくつかのLLMが条件内で無料で使用できます。（2026/7時点）

チャットウインドウ下部のモデル選択リストを選択すると表示されるモデルで、「（SAP Build）」と付与されているものが、事前定義済みLLMです。
2026/7/6現在ですが、Claude 4.5 SonnetとClaude 4.6 Sonnetがリストされます。
利用条件としては下記のように最大トークン数等細かい条件は明記されないものの、無料で使用できるということがメリットです。

  • Model availability may be updated or limited.

  • AI model usage is subject to per-user fair-use limits.

  • SAP may adjust usage limits or restrict access to models or features.

上記の制限に該当して機能が利用できなくなった場合や、Sonnet以外のLLMを使用したい場合はLLMとして外部のLLM プロバイダや、SAP AI Coreに対して接続してそれらのLLMを利用することも可能です。SAP AI Coreに接続すればGPTやOpus等より高推論のモデルを使用する事ができます。（注：この形式の場合は接続先のサービスに対して課金が発生します。

例えば、Sonnetも悪くはないのですが、SAP AI Coreに接続し、プランをOpus等の高推論モデルで行い、実行を無料の事前定義済みモデルのSonnetで行うといったコスト削減のテクニックも利用できます。

この機能はSAP CAP,SAP UI5,SAP FioriのMCPサーバもディフォルトで設定されています。


SAP Build Codeに前からあったJoule for Developerの機能と違い、CAPのアプリケーションを作成する場合は
「SAP CAPで〜」
という形でプロンプトを入力する必要がありますが、BTP上で動作するアプリケーションを作成する事ができます。Joule for Developerはコマンドで行うことを明確に示す必要がありましたが、このチャット機能はプロンプトで行いたいこと、やらせたいことを指示する必要があります。

Joule for Developerより良い点としてはデバッグです。エラーが発生した場合、そのエラーを貼り付けることで解決に向けたアクションを行ってくれるということです。
※既存のSAP Build Codeプロジェクトに対してもこの機能を利用することが出来ます。Joule for Developerを使用して開発したプロジェクトに対してもチャット機能で機能追加やデバッグも可能です。

現在のところ無料で利用することが出来ますので、ぜひとも試してみて下さい。

entry.title: Re: How to obtain data passed from an Odata (v4) RAP application list report
entry.content:
Hello HarryElliott,

Since you are using RAP and you want to calculate the aggregated data for all employees, first thing to understood here is that Fiori Element Framework uses pagination concept so you would only see first 20 records and you need to scroll to the end so that data of all employees are received.

Then only you would be able to do the calculation and such calculation at frontend is not the right approach from performance perspective.

The one solution would be to create a Function in your RAP like "GetAnnualLeaveForAllEmployee" and inside that implementation you can get the result from CDS which is aggregating the data.

Another solution would be to create URI which does aggregation using SUM and GROUP and bind to the custom UI5 Control.

entry.title: Sales Order to AR Invoice query
entry.content:
i want result as document level in one line. but in my query it shows itemwise. please help.

 

SELECT
   
    T0.DocEntry,
    
CASE
   WHEN T2.DocDate IS NOT NULL
   THEN CAST(YEAR(T2.DocDate) AS VARCHAR(4)) + '-' +
        RIGHT(CAST(YEAR(T2.DocDate)+1 AS VARCHAR(4)),2)
   ELSE ''
END AS [Invoice FY],
 
    T4.SeriesName,
    T0.DocNum AS [SO No],
    T0.DocDate AS [SO Date],
    T0.CardCode as [Customer Code],
    T0.CardName as [Customer Name],
 
CASE T0.U_Type   
WHEN 1 THEN 'Sales'
WHEN 2 THEN 'Service'
WHEN 3 THEN 'Blank'
WHEN 4 THEN 'Adv-PO-Sales'
WHEN 5 THEN 'Adv-PO-Service'
WHEN 6 THEN 'Sales - Life Sciences'
WHEN 7 THEN 'Service - Life Sciences'
WHEN 8 THEN 'Sales - FD'
WHEN 9 THEN 'Service - FD'
WHEN 10 THEN 'Sales - MSF'
WHEN 11 THEN 'Service - MSF'
END AS'Type',
 
    T0.U_Region as [Region],
    Sum((T1.[Price]*T1.Quantity)) as 'SO Total',
 
CASE 
WHEN T0.DocStatus = 'O' AND T0.CANCELED = 'N'
    THEN Sum((T1.[Price]*T1.Quantity)) ELSE 0 END AS [Open SO Amount],
 
CASE 
WHEN T0.DocStatus = 'C' AND T0.CANCELED = 'N'
    THEN Sum((T1.[Price]*T1.Quantity)) ELSE 0 END AS [Closed SO Amount],
 
CASE 
WHEN T0.CANCELED = 'Y'
    THEN Sum((T1.[Price]*T1.Quantity)) ELSE 0 END AS [Cancelled SO Amount],
 
--CASE    
--     WHEN T0.CANCELED = 'Y' THEN 'Cancelled'    
--     WHEN T0.DocStatus = 'C' THEN 'Closed'    
--     WHEN T0.DocStatus = 'O' THEN 'Open'    
--ELSE 'Unknown'END AS [SO Status],
 
    T2.DocNum AS [Invoice No],
 
--CASE 
-- WHEN T2.CANCELED = 'N'
--    THEN Sum((T3.[Price]*T3.Quantity))
--END As 'Inv Total'
 
    T2.DocDate AS [Invoice Date],
Sum((T3.[Price]*T3.Quantity)) as 'INV Total'
 
--CASE    
--     WHEN T2.CANCELED = 'Y' THEN 'Cancelled'    
--     WHEN T2.DocStatus = 'C' THEN 'Closed'    
--     WHEN T2.DocStatus = 'O' THEN 'Open'    
--ELSE 'Unknown'END AS [Invoice Status]
 
 
 
FROM ORDR T0
INNER JOIN RDR1 T1 ON T0.DocEntry = T1.DocEntry
LEFT OUTER  JOIN dln1 T3A ON T1.DocEntry = T3A.BaseEntry AND T1.LineNum  = T3A.BaseLine AND T3A.ItemCode=T1.ItemCode
LEFT OUTER  JOIN INV1 T3  ON T3."BaseEntry" = (case when T3."BaseType"='17' then T1.DocEntry when T3."BaseType"='15' then T3A.DocEntry else 0 end)  
AND T3."BaseLine" = (case when T3."BaseType"='17' then T1.LineNum else T3A.LineNum end) 
AND T3."ItemCode" = (case when T3."BaseType"='17' then T1.ItemCode else T3A.ItemCode end) 
LEFT OUTER JOIN OINV T2 ON T3.DocEntry = T2.DocEntry
LEFT outer  JOIN NNM1 T4 ON T0.Series = T4.Series
LEFT JOIN RDN1 T5    ON T5.BaseType = 15   AND T5.BaseEntry = T3A.DocEntry   AND T5.BaseLine = T3A.LineNum
 
Where 
--T0.DocDate BETWEEN [%0] AND [%1] and (T2.docDate IS NULL OR T2.DocDate <= [%1])
T0.DocEntry = '71380'
and T5.DocEntry IS NULL
--and T0.CANCELED = 'Y'
--and (T2.CANCELED = 'N')
 
Group By T1.Price,
T1.Quantity,
T3.[Price],T3.Quantity,
T1.DiscPrcnt,
T0.DocNum,    
T0.DocDate,    
T2.DocNum,    
T2.DocDate,    
T0.CANCELED,    
T2.CANCELED,    
T0.DocStatus,    
T2.DocStatus,    
T0.CardCode,
T0.CardName,
T0.U_Type,
T0.U_Region,
T0.DocEntry,
T4.SeriesName
 
 
ORDER BY 
    T0.DocDate,
    T0.DocNum,
    T0.CardCode

entry.title: Can SAP Joule Skills Be Triggered by External Events (e.g., Incoming Email)?
entry.content:
I would like to understand whether SAP Joule Skills can be triggered automatically based on external events, rather than being invoked manually by a user.

For example, consider the following scenario:

  • An email arrives in a shared mailbox (e.g., Outlook or Microsoft 365).
  • This email should automatically trigger a Joule Skill.
  • The skill processes the email content, performs the required business logic, and optionally creates or updates business objects in an SAP system.

Is this type of event-driven invocation currently supported for Joule Skills?

entry.title: Unlock EarlyWatch Alert Reports for SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll—Anytime, Anywhere
entry.content:
Keeping your payroll systems healthy isn’t optional—it’s critical. If you’re working with Employee Central Payroll, the EarlyWatch Alert service in SAP for Me gives you a powerful, centralized way to diagnose system performance, detect risks early, and stay ahead of issues.

This guide walks you through how to access and use this effectively for Employee Central Payroll systems.

Getting Started: Accessing SAP EarlyWatch Alert in SAP for Me

Start by logging on to the SAP for Me portal:
https://me.sap.com/home

Once you're in:

  • Click the Customize Home Page icon in the upper-left corner
  • Search the App Catalog for “EarlyWatch” after clicking on Add Card

You’ll see several applications—each designed for a different level of insight.

Key SAP EarlyWatch Alert Applications

Here’s a quick breakdown of what each app offers:

1. SAP EarlyWatch Alert Reports (Viewer)

This app lets you dive deep into detailed service reports for specific systems.

  • View full report content
  • Navigate through report sections

Perfect when you need granular analysis of a system’s health.

2. SAP EarlyWatch Alert Workspace

Think of this as your starting point for EarlyWatch Alert.

  • Overview of your entire system landscape
  • Aggregated EarlyWatch results across systems

Ideal for getting a big-picture view at a glance.

3. SAP EarlyWatch Alert Dashboard

Focused on KPIs that matter most:

  • System-specific performance indicators
  • Drill-down into historical trends

Great for tracking performance over time and spotting patterns early.

4. SAP EarlyWatch Alert Solution Finder

Need to work on the list of issues or investigate a specific issue ?

  • Perform full-text searches across all EarlyWatch reports
  • Quickly locate references to systems, errors, or topics

This is your go-to tool for root cause exploration and solution finding.

Filtering for Employee Central Payroll Systems

To access the Employee Central Payroll content, use the Global Filters available in all EarlyWatch apps:

  • Product → SFSF GLOBAL PAYROLL 
  • Product Version:
    • SFSF GLOBAL PAYROLL 1.0 → for ECC systems
    • SFSF GLOBAL PAYROLL 2.0  → for S/4HANA systems

These filters ensure you’re only seeing relevant payroll system insights, saving time.

 

Why This Matters

EarlyWatch Alert service does not just generate weekly reports —it is a preventive tool. Used consistently, the alerts triggered can help you:

  • Detect performance bottlenecks early
  • Maintain system stability
  • Improve payroll processing reliability
  • Support proactive system maintenance

If you’re responsible for Employee Central Payroll, mastering EarlyWatch in SAP for Me is a practical step toward better system oversight. With the right apps and filters in place, you’ll move from reactive troubleshooting to proactive system management.

Also refer to the SAP Note 2520319 - How to access the SAP EarlyWatch Alert apps in SAP for Me.

entry.title: Re: Profile icon on Fiori Screen not working for Macbook...
entry.content:
Hello,

After reviewing the query, we would like to share the following information regarding supported environments for SAPUI5.

According to the official SAPUI5 documentation, only the latest two major versions of macOS and the latest version of Safari are officially supported. You may refer to the support matrix here:
https://ui5.sap.com/#/topic/74b59efa0eef48988d3b716bd0ecc933

To help us investigate further, please take the following steps:

1. Verify your macOS and Safari versions
Please confirm that both your macOS version and Safari version fall within the supported scope listed in the documentation above.

2. Check and update your SAPUI5 library version
Please verify the current SAPUI5 library version on your ABAP system. If it is not on the latest version, we recommend updating it, as this may resolve the issue.

To check the current version, refer to SAP Note 2363155
To upgrade the library, refer to SAP Note 2346367
Please share your findings with us so we can continue the investigation. Do not hesitate to reach out if you have any questions.

Best regards,
Joe

entry.title: Can the requester specify/select an ad-hoc approver when creating a Supplier Invoice (Parked as Comp
entry.content:
*Title:** Can the requester specify/select an ad-hoc approver when creating a Supplier Invoice (Parked as Complete Workflow / WS00800303) in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition?

**Body:**

We are implementing the Flexible Workflow for Supplier Invoices (Workflow for Parked Invoice as Complete, WS00800303) in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

In the "Manage Workflows for Supplier Invoices" app, we understand that approvers are determined by pre-configured rules (Role, Manager of Creator, Cost Center Responsible, specific User, or Team).

Our question: **Is it possible for the requester (the person creating the supplier invoice) to manually select/specify an approver at the time the invoice is created/submitted, similar to the "Ad-hoc Approver" functionality available for Purchase Orders in the "Manage Purchase Orders" app?**

Specifically:
1. Does the Supplier Invoice flexible workflow scenario (WS00800303 / WS00800251) support an ad-hoc approver step where the initiator can pick a user at runtime?
2. If not available out-of-the-box, is there a supported extension point (e.g., BAdI MRM_WORKFLOW_AGENTS) to let the invoice creator choose the approver dynamically?
3. Is this on SAP's roadmap, given that Ad-hoc Approver already exists for PO workflows?

Any confirmation from SAP or real-world configuration experience would be appreciated. Thank you!

entry.title: Re: Implementing Row-Level Security using ABAC in SAP Databricks
entry.content:
Its possible.

Example authorizations:
USERID KEYACCOUNT MARKETSEGMENT SALESOFFICE
PAUL100**
PAUL**SO123
WHERE
(
  KEYACCOUNT = '100'
)
OR
(
  SALESOFFICE = 'SO123'
)

 

In Databricks ABAC, the row filter UDF can look up the authorization table and check whether at least one rule exists that matches the current row:
EXISTS (
  SELECT 1
  FROM USER_AUTH A
  WHERE A.USERID = CURRENT_USER()

  AND (
       (A.KEYACCOUNT IS NULL OR A.KEYACCOUNT = SALES.KEYACCOUNT)
   AND (A.MARKETSEGMENT IS NULL OR A.MARKETSEGMENT = SALES.MARKETSEGMENT)
   AND (A.SALESOFFICE IS NULL OR A.SALESOFFICE = SALES.SALESOFFICE)
  )
)

 You need to consider this below 

  1. Store authorizations in a dedicated table.
  2. Pass the current user into a row-filter function.
  3. Evaluate the authorization table dynamically.
  4. Return TRUE if any authorization rule matches (OR logic).

entry.title: Re: connection between SAP Datasphere and Microsoft Fabric
entry.content:
No, there is no native, direct "Microsoft Fabric" connection type listed inside SAP Datasphere. However, you can absolutely achieve your customer's goal of connecting SAP Datasphere to the Azure SQL Database that is being mirrored into Microsoft Fabric.

Check this out : 

#https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/fabric/mirroring/sap-datasphere-tutorial

entry.title: Suggestion Required for Managing Enquiry Process
entry.content:
Dear Team,

We would like your suggestion regarding the enquiry process in SAP Business One.

Currently, for every enquiry, a Lead or Customer Code needs to be created. However, many enquiries do not result in confirmed orders, which leads to the creation of a large number of unnecessary Lead/Customer Codes in the system.

Our requirement is to manage enquiries without creating a Lead or Customer Code initially. Once the customer confirms the order, we would like to enter the customer details and create the Customer Code at the order booking stage.

Could you please suggest the best practice or standard process in SAP Business One to handle this requirement? If there is any recommended approach to avoid creating unnecessary customer codes while still managing enquiries effectively, please let us know.

Looking forward to your guidance.

Thanks & Regards,

entry.title: Accelerate Outbound Processing with Automatic Picking Warehouse Task Creation
entry.content:
In modern warehouse operations speed and efficiency are critical. Every outbound warehouse request must be processed quickly so warehouse operatives can begin picking without unnecessary delays.

To help customers automate repetitive processes, SAP Logistics Management now introduces Automatic Picking Warehouse Task Creation.

By defining configurable schedule workloads, logistics clerks can automatically create picking warehouse tasks for eligible outbound warehouse requests, reducing manual effort while ensuring warehouse operations remain transparent and under control.

 

From Manual Processing to Automated Warehouse Execution

Every outbound warehouse request represents work that eventually needs to be picked. Before warehouse operatives can begin, picking warehouse tasks must be created.

Automatic Picking Warehouse Task Creation eliminates this repetitive step. Instead of manually triggering task creation, the system continuously evaluates outbound warehouse requests based on configurable workloads and automatically creates picking warehouse tasks whenever the defined conditions are met.

As a result, warehouse picking can start sooner, while logistics clerks can focus on handling exceptions instead of routine operational work.

 

Schedule Flexible Workload

At the center of this capability is the new Schedule Workload Creation application, where logistics clerks can configure and manage automation schedules for each warehouse.

Using this application, clerks can:

  • Enable workload automation for individual warehouses
  • Define one-time or recurring execution schedules
  • Configure business-specific selection criteria
  • Maintain multiple schedules to support different operational scenarios

Automation rules can be tailored using business attributes such as:

  • Planned Goods Issue (GI) date
  • Ship-to party
  • Carrier
  • Request creation date
  • Staging bin

Schedule Workload Creation Application

This flexibility allows clerks to automate picking based on their own operational priorities without changing warehouse processes.

 

Automatic Picking Warehouse Task Creation

Once an automation schedule is active, the system periodically evaluates outbound warehouse requests based on the configured selection criteria.

For every eligible request, SAP Logistics Management automatically creates the corresponding picking warehouse tasks. Once created, these tasks become immediately available in the Process Warehouse Tasks application, where logistics clerks can review, organize, and continue managing warehouse execution. Also they are immediately available in the SAP Warehouse Logistics mobile app where warehouser operatives can start execution.

Monitor Job Schedules Application

This seamless transition from automated task creation to warehouse execution helps reduce manual effort while ensuring picking activities can begin without unnecessary delays.

 

Transparent Monitoring and Execution Tracking

Automation should simplify warehouse operations—not create a "black box."

For this reason, every automation execution is recorded in Monitor Job Schedules, giving logistics clerks full visibility into the automation process.

Execution logs include information such as:

  • The automation schedule that was executed
  • Trigger type
  • Processing results
  • Error details for failed items

With built-in monitoring, warehouse teams can quickly review execution results, investigate issues, and verify that outbound requests have been processed successfully—all without requiring technical expertise.

Process Warehouse Tasks Application

 

Outlook: Building the Foundation for Smarter Warehouse Automation

Automatic Picking Warehouse Task Creation is an important step toward more automation in SAP Logistics Management.

Looking ahead, we could expand this automation framework to support additional warehouse workloads and further streamline warehouse execution through capabilities such as task bundling and work distribution. In addition, we are planning to work on more stock removal strategies (currently supported: FIFO and fixed bin) in order to allocate the best matching stock.

Beyond warehouse execution, we also see opportunities to expand this automation approach to other logistics processes, supporting a more connected and automated logistics landscape across SAP Logistics Management.

 

Call to Action

Your questions and comments are welcome! Please share your feedback and thoughts in a comment.

Follow me on the SAP Community to stay up to date!

If you are interested in Logistics Management’s other services, I recommend you read Logistics Management Help Document, and your questions will be answered here.

entry.title: Re: Responsibility Management: Create Your Rules (5/5)

I found this post as well for Public cloud https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-q-a/unified-responsibility-management-is-not-activated-for-flexible-workflow/qaq-p/14039968 but it has not been anwsered.

entry.title: Re: BADI To maintain Header text of the PO at the backend
entry.content:
Compare your requirement with the solutions provided in

  • (KBA) 3524757 - How to include a Standard Text on a Purchase Order form 
  • (FAQ) 448814 - FAQ: Texts in Purchasing 

If neither Customizing (text copy rules) nor form adaptation resolves the issue, evaluate the use of BAdI MMPUR_PO_STD_HEADER_TEXT as an alternative solution.

entry.title: MRP Run for FG Material Considers Supply and Demand from Other WBS Elements
entry.content:
Issue Description:

We are executing MRP for a Finished Goods (FG) material assigned to a specific WBS element.

Business Requirement:
When MRP is run for the FG material with a selected WBS, the system should generate procurement proposals (Purchase Requisitions and/or Production Orders) only for that WBS. The planning should be restricted to the selected WBS and should not consider supply or demand from other WBS elements.

Current Behavior:
After executing MRP for the FG material, the system considers dependent requirements, planned orders, and production orders belonging to other WBS elements while calculating the procurement proposals. As a result, the generated procurement proposals are influenced by other WBS elements instead of being planned independently for the selected WBS.

Expected Behavior:
The MRP run should be restricted to the selected WBS so that procurement proposals are generated exclusively for that WBS.

In SAP ECC and SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud/On-Premise, this requirement can be achieved using transaction MD51.

entry.title: Does an SAP PP Consultant Need to Know This?
entry.content:
I am currently working as an SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud PP consultant. Since my experience is focused on the Public Cloud, I am not deeply familiar with ECC 6.0 or Private Cloud environments—meaning I am not as accustomed to traditional T-codes. While studying, I naturally came across lectures covering ECC and S/4HANA On-Premise, so I have a basic understanding, but as a Public Cloud consultant, my knowledge of the SAP PP process is heavily centered around the standard Scope Items and Best Practices provided by SAP.

 

This brings me to my question.

Right now, my expertise covers:

PP Master Data

Basic MRP logic

Creating planned orders and production orders, issuing raw/component materials, and confirming production results.

 

To be honest, I often wonder if I even know 5% of what the SAP PP module has to offer. Put simply, while I understand the core baseline processes, I lack deep knowledge of more specialized areas like PP/DS, S&OP (IBP), LTP, CRP, Variant BOM,Batch Management, and Serial Number Management. Is it absolutely necessary to know each of these advanced concepts inside out to apply them in real-world implementation projects? Also, what would be the best sequence or roadmap to study these remaining PP concepts? Because the sheer volume of SAP T-codes and Fiori apps is so vast, it is quite overwhelming to know where to start.

Thank you so much for reading this, and a huge shout-out to all the SAP consultants out there—keep more GOING!!!

entry.title: SDI replication of a table from SAP ECC to Hana Cloud, using MssqlECCAdapter
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

I'm working on a scenario where I need to replicate a table from SAP ECC to SAP HANA Cloud using Smart Data Integration with the MSSQLECCAdapter.

I have successfully tested the connection and verified that a virtual table can be created, so the SDI configuration appears to be working correctly.

My next step is to perform the initial load. For this, I'm using the Business Application Studio (BAS) editor to create and deploy an .hdbrepltask artifact. I already have a similar replication scenario running successfully from an SAP S/4HANA source using the HANAAdapter, so I initially tried to reuse the same approach.

However, when deploying the replication task for the ECC source, I run into deployment issues. The editor allows me to design and configure the replication task, but the deployment fails. Unfortunately, I have not yet been able to identify the root cause or determine whether the issue is related to the adapter, the replication task definition, or the tooling itself.

Has anyone implemented a similar setup using MSSQLECCAdapter and HANA Cloud?

I'm particularly interested in:

  • Whether .hdbrepltask artifacts are the recommended approach for this scenario.
  • Any known limitations or differences compared to using the HANAAdapter.
  • Alternative approaches you may have used for the initial load and ongoing replication.

Any insights, documentation references, or examples would be greatly appreciated.

Thanks in advance!

entry.title: Coffee Sales Predicted Everyday with SAP RPT
entry.content:
It Started with a Monday Morning Spreadsheet

It was a Monday morning. The kind where you arrive at the office before anyone else, stare at last week's sales numbers, and try to figure out how much stock to order for the week ahead. Our coffee shop manager, Priya, had been doing this for three years. Every Monday. Same spreadsheet. Same gut feeling. Same 20% over-ordering "just to be safe."

Priya knew the patterns intuitively. Hot days meant more iced lattes. Weekends meant brunch crowds. A promotion running meant foot traffic converted better. But turning that intuition into a number — a real, defensible forecast — required either a data scientist or a lot of luck. 

Then a colleague mentioned SAP RPT. "It's a foundation model for tabular data," he said. "You just send it your historical data and it predicts. No training. No model deployment. No PhD required."

Priya was sceptical. So was I. So we tested it. As I come from a technical background, building a quick prototype was quite easy. This document is the complete record of what happened, every step, every line of code, every result. By the end, you will have everything you need to run your own first prediction.

 

What you need to follow along?

1. An SAP BTP subaccount,

2. Python 3.9+ with pip, and

3. About 30 mins to 1 hour for the one-time BTP setup.

After that, each new prediction use case takes mere seconds to minutes

 

Part 1: Setting Up SAP BTP and AI Core (One-Time)

This setup is done once. Every prediction use case you build after this reuses the same service key and deployment ID.

Step 1: Log in to SAP BTP Cockpit

Navigate to https://cockpit.btp.cloud.sap and sign in.

  • Land on the Global Account overview
  • Click into your Subaccount (or create one — choose eu10 or us10 for AI Core support)

Note your Subaccount ID — you will need it for the service key

SAP BTP Global Account

Step 2: Subscribe to SAP AI Core (Extended Plan)

In your Subaccount: Services > Service Marketplace > search "SAP AI Core".

  1. Click the SAP AI Core tile
  2. Click "Create" — select the Extended plan (required for RPT foundation models)
  3. Wait 2-5 minutes for activation
  4. Verify: Services > Instances and Subscriptions shows SAP AI Core as "Subscribed"

Extended plan is mandatory.  The Free and Standard plans do not include foundation models. RPT is only available under the Extended plan. Check current plan pricing at: https://discovery-center.cloud.sap/serviceCatalog/sap-ai-core

SAP AI Core - Extended Plan

 

Step 3: Create a Service Key

The service key is a JSON file containing your OAuth credentials. Your Python code uses these to authenticate with the AI Core API.

  1. Services > Instances and Subscriptions > find your AI Core instance
  2. Click the three-dot menu > "Create Service Key"
  3. Name it (e.g., rpt-key) and click Create
  4. Click the key name > View > copy the entire JSON

The JSON structure you will receive:

{
  "clientid":     "sb-xxxxxxxx!bXXX|aicore!bXXX",
  "clientsecret": "xxxxxxxx$xxxxxxxx=",
  "url":          "https://<subdomain>.authentication.eu10.hana.ondemand.com",
  "serviceurls": {
    "AI_API_URL": "https://api.ai.prod.eu-central-1.aws.ml.hana.ondemand.com"
  }
}

 

Step 4: Find Your RPT Deployment ID

The Deployment ID identifies the running RPT model instance. SAP manages the deployment — you just need its ID.

  1. From Instances and Subscriptions, click "SAP AI Launchpad" to open it
  2. In AI Launchpad: ML Operations > Deployments
  3. Find a deployment with Scenario = "foundation-models" and Status = "Running"
  4. Copy the Deployment ID (16-character hex, e.g., dbff87ddae58361d)

No deployments visible?  Go to ML Operations > Configurations > Create a new configuration for the "foundation-models" scenario. Then deploy it from ML Operations > Deployments > Create. Takes about 5 minutes.

Select RPT model from Model Library in SAP AI Launchpad

 

Access the Deployment URL

 

Part 2: The Use Case — Coffee Shop Daily Sales

We predict daily coffee shop revenue from four observable features. This use case was chosen because the relationships are intuitive: hot days drive iced drink sales, weekends bring leisure customers, promotions convert foot traffic. You can sanity-check the predictions by eye.

Feature

Type

Description

Example

temp_f

Numeric

Outside temperature in degrees Fahrenheit

74

is_weekend

Binary (0/1)

1 = Saturday or Sunday, 0 = weekday

1

promotions

Integer

Number of active deals or discount offers

2

foot_traffic

Integer

Estimated pedestrians passing the shop

850

daily_sales

Numeric — TARGET

Total revenue in USD for the day

1,250

 We have 35 days of historical data covering different seasons, temperatures, and promotion levels. We want to predict sales for 5 upcoming days. The test rows have daily_sales = "[Predict]" — the sentinel value that tells RPT which rows to fill in.

 

Part 3: Setting Up Jupyter Notebook

All the code in this guide runs inside a Jupyter Notebook. Jupyter lets you run Python code cell by cell, see outputs immediately, and keep your credentials and results in one place. If you already have Jupyter installed, skip to Step 5.

Install Jupyter

Open a terminal (macOS/Linux) or Command Prompt (Windows) and run:

pip install jupyter notebook

# Or if you use conda:
conda install -c conda-forge notebook

Launch Jupyter Notebook

  1. In your terminal, navigate to your project folder:
cd ~/projects/sap-rpt-demo
  1. Start the Jupyter server:
jupyter notebook

Your browser will open automatically at http://localhost:8888. If it does not, copy the URL shown in the terminal (it includes a token).

Create a New Notebook

  1. Click "New" in the top-right corner
  2. Select "Python 3 (ipykernel)"
  3. A new notebook opens with an empty cell — you are ready to code
  4. Rename the notebook: click "Untitled" at the top and type "SAP_RPT_CoffeeShop"

How Jupyter cells work:  Each grey box is a cell. Type code into a cell and press Shift+Enter to run it. The output appears directly below. The cell number (e.g., In [1]:) increments each time you run a cell. All variables persist across cells in the same session.

 

Part 4: The Python Code — Each Cell in Your Notebook

Copy each cell below into your Jupyter notebook in order. Run each cell with Shift+Enter before moving to the next. The In [N]: header shows the cell number and the Out [N]: line shows the output.

Cell 1: Install Dependencies

In [1]:
import sys
!{sys.executable} -m pip install requests pandas --quiet
import requests, pandas as pd, json
print('Libraries ready.')
Out [1]: Libraries ready.

Security note:  Never commit credentials to source control. Use environment variables or a secrets manager. In production: DEPLOYMENT_ID = os.environ["RPT_DEPLOYMENT_ID"]

Cell 2: Configure Credentials

Paste the values from your BTP service key (Step 3) and deployment ID (Step 4):

In [2]:
# Paste from your BTP service key
AI_CORE_AUTH_URL      = 'https://<subdomain>.authentication.eu10.hana.ondemand.com'
AI_CORE_CLIENT_ID     = 'sb-<uuid>!b<n>|aicore!b<n>'
AI_CORE_CLIENT_SECRET = '<uuid>$<secret>='
AI_CORE_API_BASE      = 'https://api.ai.prod.eu-central-1.aws.ml.hana.ondemand.com'
DEPLOYMENT_ID         = '<your-16-char-id>'
RESOURCE_GROUP        = 'default'
print('Configuration loaded.')
Out [2]: Configuration loaded.

Cell 3: Get an OAuth Token

SAP AI Core uses OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials. The token is valid for 12 hours. Cache it and reuse it — do not request a new token per prediction call.

In [3]:	
r = requests.post(
    f'{AI_CORE_AUTH_URL}/oauth/token',
    data={'grant_type': 'client_credentials'},
    auth=(AI_CORE_CLIENT_ID, AI_CORE_CLIENT_SECRET),
    timeout=30
)
r.raise_for_status()
TOKEN = r.json()['access_token']
print(f'Token valid for {r.json()["expires_in"] // 3600} hours.')
Out [3]: Token valid for 12 hours.

Cell 4: Build the Combined Dataset

The combined dataset pattern is the core of how RPT works. Training rows (with real labels) and test rows (with "[Predict]") are sent together in a single list. RPT learns from the labelled rows and fills in the placeholder rows. We use 35 training days covering cold winter days, mild spring days, hot summer days, and everything in between.

In [4]:
TARGET      = 'daily_sales'
PLACEHOLDER = '[Predict]'
COLS        = ['temp_f', 'is_weekend', 'promotions', 'foot_traffic']
ALL_COLS    = COLS + [TARGET]

# 35 days of historical data (varied seasons and conditions)
train_features = [
    # Mild weekdays
    [68,0,1,620],[72,0,1,650],[65,0,0,580],[70,0,1,610],[67,0,0,590],
    [71,0,1,630],[69,0,0,600],[73,0,2,660],[66,0,1,570],[64,0,0,550],
    # Warm/hot weekdays
    [80,0,2,720],[85,0,2,750],[88,0,1,700],[90,0,3,780],[92,0,2,760],
    [95,0,3,800],[83,0,1,710],[87,0,2,740],[84,0,0,690],[89,0,2,770],
    # Weekends (mild to hot)
    [75,1,2,920],[80,1,2,980],[85,1,3,1050],[88,1,3,1100],[78,1,2,950],
    [82,1,2,990],[90,1,3,1150],[92,1,3,1200],[76,1,2,930],[83,1,2,1000],
    # Cold days
    [45,0,0,420],[48,0,1,450],[50,1,1,580],[52,1,2,620],[55,0,1,500],
]
train_labels = [
    # Mild weekdays
    820,870,760,800,790,850,780,890,750,720,
    # Warm/hot weekdays
    950,1020,980,1080,1050,1120,960,1010,940,1060,
    # Weekends
    1150,1220,1320,1380,1180,1250,1450,1500,1160,1280,
    # Cold days
    560,590,720,780,650,
]
# 5 upcoming days to predict
test_features = [
    [74,0,1,640],    # Wednesday, mild, 1 promo
    [86,1,2,1020],   # Saturday, hot, 2 promos
    [91,1,3,1180],   # Sunday, very hot, 3 promos
    [47,0,0,430],    # Cold Tuesday, no promo
    [79,1,3,1050],   # Warm Saturday, 3 promos
]
all_records = (
    [dict(zip(ALL_COLS, f+[l])) for f,l in zip(train_features,train_labels)] +
    [dict(zip(ALL_COLS, f+[PLACEHOLDER])) for f in test_features]
)
print(f'Dataset: {len(all_records)} rows ({len(train_features)} train + {len(test_features)} predict)')
Out [4]: Dataset: 40 rows (35 train + 5 predict)

Cell 5: Call the RPT API

The payload schema is strict. The prediction_placeholder value in target_columns must exactly match the value used in the rows.

In [5]:
url  = f'{AI_CORE_API_BASE}/v2/inference/deployments/{DEPLOYMENT_ID}/predict'
hdrs = {
    'Authorization':     f'Bearer {TOKEN}',
    'AI-Resource-Group': RESOURCE_GROUP,
    'Content-Type':      'application/json'
}
payload = {
    'prediction_config': {
        'target_columns': [
            {'name': TARGET, 'prediction_placeholder': PLACEHOLDER}
        ]
    },
    'rows': all_records
}
resp = requests.post(url, headers=hdrs, json=payload, timeout=60)
print(f'HTTP {resp.status_code}', 'OK' if resp.status_code == 200 else resp.text[:200])
Out [5]: HTTP 200 OK

Cell 6: Parse the Response

The RPT response nests predictions four levels deep: outer dict > list > inner dict > "prediction" key. The prediction value is a string — cast it to float.

In [6]:
preds_raw = resp.json()['predictions']

def to_scalar(v):
    if isinstance(v, dict): v = v.get(TARGET, list(v.values())[0])
    while isinstance(v, (list, tuple)) and len(v) > 0: v = v[0]
    if isinstance(v, dict): v = v.get('prediction', list(v.values())[0])
    return float(v)

def to_confidence(v):
    if isinstance(v, dict): v = v.get(TARGET, list(v.values())[0])
    while isinstance(v, (list, tuple)) and len(v) > 0: v = v[0]
    return float(v.get('confidence', 0)) if isinstance(v, dict) else 0.0

preds = [to_scalar(p) for p in preds_raw][-len(test_features):]
confs = [to_confidence(p) for p in preds_raw][-len(test_features):]

days = [
    'Wednesday (74F, mild, 1 promo)',
    'Saturday  (86F, hot, 2 promos)',
    'Sunday    (91F, very hot, 3 promos)',
    'Tuesday   (47F, cold, no promo)',
    'Saturday  (79F, warm, 3 promos)',
]
print('=== RPT Predictions ===')
for day, pred, conf in zip(days, preds, confs):
    print(f'{day:<40} -> ${pred:>7,.0f}   confidence={conf:.2f}')
Out [6]: === RPT Predictions ===
Wednesday (74F, mild, 1 promo)           ->    $870   confidence=0.74
Saturday  (86F, hot, 2 promos)           ->  $1,290   confidence=0.86
Sunday    (91F, very hot, 3 promos)      ->  $1,490   confidence=0.92
Tuesday   (47F, cold, no promo)          ->    $575   confidence=0.81
Saturday  (79F, warm, 3 promos)          ->  $1,180   confidence=0.88

 

Part 4: Results and What They Tell Us

Running the code above against a live SAP AI Core RPT deployment produces the following predictions:

Day

Temp (F)

Weekend

Promos

Foot Traffic

RPT Prediction

Confidence

Wednesday

74

No

1

640

$870

0.74

Saturday

86

Yes

2

1,020

$1,290

0.86

Sunday

91

Yes

3

1,180

$1,490

0.92

Tuesday

47

No

0

430

$575

0.81

Saturday

79

Yes

3

1,050

$1,180

0.88

The predictions are directionally correct and internally consistent. Sunday (hottest, most promotions, highest foot traffic, weekend) gets the highest prediction. Cold Tuesday (no promotion, low foot traffic) gets the lowest. The confidence scores reflect pattern strength — Sunday at 0.92 closely matches many similar hot weekend days in the training data, while Wednesday at 0.74 is a more average day with fewer strong signals.

 

Part 5: Full Sample Data Table — All 40 Rows

The table below shows every row sent to the RPT API. Rows 1-35 are training data with known sales values covering mild weekdays, warm/hot weekdays, weekends, and cold days. Rows 36-40 had daily_sales = "[Predict]" in the request. The green values are what RPT returned.

Rows 1-35 are training data with actual sales values covering four weather and day-type categories. Rows 36-40 were sent with daily_sales = "[Predict]". The green values are what RPT returned, along with confidence scores.

Conclusion: From Zero to Production in One Afternoon

Priya ran the code on a Tuesday morning. By the time her second coffee was ready, she had predictions for the next five days — with confidence scores telling her how certain the model was. The Sunday prediction came back at $1,490 with 0.92 confidence. She ordered accordingly. She was right within 4%.

That is the promise of SAP RPT. Not magic. Not a replacement for human judgment. But a fast, cheap, accurate starting point that takes hours instead of months to set up.

Here is what you accomplished in this guide:

  1. Set up SAP BTP and subscribed to AI Core Extended plan
  2. Created a service key and obtained OAuth credentials
  3. Located the RPT deployment ID in AI Launchpad
  4. Set up Jupyter Notebook and organised all code into runnable cells
  5. Built a 40-row combined dataset with "[Predict]" placeholders
  6. Called the RPT API and received predictions with confidence scores
  7. Parsed the four-level nested response structure

The coffee shop example is intentionally simple. The same code with different column names and different data works for sales forecasting, customer churn prediction, demand planning, fraud scoring, equipment failure prediction, and any other tabular regression or classification task your organisation needs.

Summary: What RPT Predicted for the Coffee Shop

I would love your feedback!  Did this guide help you get your first RPT prediction running? Was anything unclear, missing, or could be explained better? Drop a comment below or reach out directly, trust me, your feedback shapes future guides. Even a quick "worked for me" or "stuck at Step X" is incredibly helpful. Thank you for reading.

entry.title: How to extract multi references from XML Bank Statements
entry.content:
Hi community,

I'm looking for a way to extract multiple invoice numbers from an unstructured text to use for payment matching.
In SAP Note https://me.sap.com/notes/3055456 it mentions "When you have imported a bank statement with multiple reference numbers, that is, multiple numbers with commas in between in the <Ustrd> field of the bank file, those numbers will now be separated according to the commas as multiple rows in the Multiple Payments window."

My challenge is how to extract the reference numbers and bring the information in the comma separated format!?
We receive often single payments for various invoices.
Do you have a best practise? I only manage to extract single reference numbers with regular expressions.

Background is the switch from MT940 format to camt053 XML. My hope was that more structured camt053 will make use of official payment reference fields, but reality is that the information is still hidden in unstructured text in Ustrd field. It contains dates, values and invoice numbers.

How can I extract all found invoice numbers? Is there some kind of loop possibility or recursion in Electronic File Manager?

For example

RmtInf>
<Ustrd>/INV/631240 20.4.2026/INV/631263 21.4.2026</Ustrd>
</RmtInf>
should extract to 631240, 631263

Thanks very much

entry.title: Regarding unexpected behavior in batch input for ME12
entry.content:
We are developing an add-on program for SAP S/4HANA.
We are using an add-on program for the mass maintenance of purchasing info records, but it is exhibiting strange behavior. I would appreciate your advice on the cause and potential solutions.

The screen operations were performed in the following order:

#1. Updated purchasing info record for vendor XXXX in ME12 (updated price) by manual.
#2. a few minutes later. Updated purchasing info record for vendor YYYY in ME12 (updated price) by manual
#3. a few minutes later.Attempted to update purchasing info records (prices) for multiple items for vendor ZZZZ using the mass maintenance add-on.

For the first data record in step #3, the following error appeared:
"Supplier differs from supplier XXXX from the info record"
(Message Class 06, Message No. 314)
This unexpected behavior resulted in an error involving a vendor that was not being used at all in step #3.
We have been unable to intentionally reproduce the issue (it seems to happen unpredictably).

I would appreciate your guidance regarding the cause and how to address this.
Thank you in advance.

entry.title: Cloud ALM: Clarification on Access groups auto provisioning in BPMON application
entry.content:
Hi Team,

Currently, we are enabling BPMON functionality in Cloud ALM for business processes monitoring.

As we all know, we must follow two-layer security model to access business KPIs. One is at BTP subaccount level to provision role collections for restricting  and second is at BPMON application level to provision access groups.

Our concern is about automate access provisioning. Role collections provisioning, we can automate by using CIS User groups or APIs .

But we would need assistance on how to automate “Access Groups” provisioning to users in BPMON application. As we wanted to monitor business data based on legal entity(Organizational values) and will be creating Access groups for each Org. values based on KPIs, We should required to have feasibility for creating or assigning BPMON Access Groups.

We came to know that SAP does not currently provide a method/tools/options OR official public APIs for creating or assigning BPMON Access Groups.

Thus, please check and assist us how can we automate access groups provisioning.

Regards

Madhu

entry.title: What's New in SAP Integrated Product Development: May 2026
entry.content:
What’s New for SAP Integrated Product Development

Welcome to the May 2026 blog! This update introduces powerful enhancements designed to accelerate innovation, strengthen collaboration, and improve efficiency across the product lifecycle. With expanded engineering capabilities and deeper integration with SAP and partner solutions, it further enhances how teams work together—from concept through manufacturing and beyond.

Change Management

Default Status Profiles for Change Requests and Change Implementations 

As part of the standard shipment, predefined status profiles are provided for the Change Request and Change Implementation sections, each containing a set of predefined statuses.

 [Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

To learn more about the feature, see Managing Statuses for Change Management.

Updates to Relevance and Implementation Action in Change Management Apps

The Change Management apps are now available to simplify and streamline the user experience.

Key enhancements include:
• Removal of the “Default Relevance” column from the Configure Change Process Types app
• Removal of the “Implementation Action” column from the Change Request and Change Implementation tabs.
• Removal of the “Relevance” column from the Change Implementation section in the Manage Change Process app
• Updated “Relevance” values in the Change Request section of the Manage Change Process app:

  • New Version
  • Reference
  • Not Relevant
  • Changebbg

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

To learn more about the feature, see Managing Change Validities, Problem Reports, and Change Requests.

Default Status Profiles in Configure Change Process Types

The Configure Change Process Types app now includes the new default status profiles: DFLT_CR_SP and DFLT_CI_SP which are automatically preselected for change request and change implementation processes and can no longer be modified.

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

To learn more about the feature, see Configuring Change Process Types.

Common Functions 

Authorization contexts for test management 

 Our Test Management SaaS application now allows administrators to manage access restrictions for users and teams directly within the Manage Authorization Contexts app.

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

 New app for managing currency exchange rates

A new app, Manage Currency Exchange Rates, is introduced as an administrative application for managing predefined currency exchange rates with Excel import capabilities and automated timeline calculations. You can now maintain consistent, controlled exchange rates for business planning while avoiding real-time rate fluctuations.

To learn more about the feature, see Managing Currency Exchange Rates.

Authorization contexts for design items 

Our Product Data Integration SaaS application now allows administrators to manage access restrictions for users and teams directly within the Manage Authorization Contexts app.

 [Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

To learn more about the feature, see Managing Authorization Contexts.

Formulation

 New calculation type: Estimated Price

  • Administrators can now configure calculations using the new Estimated Price calculation type, which allows product developers to estimate price for a certain primary output. The price is determined based on manually defined unit prices for the formula items.
  • A new Currencies card is now available in the Synchronize Data for Formulation app, allowing administrators to upload currency data. This data is required for maintaining price calculations.

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

To learn more about the feature, see Configuring Recipe Calculations.

Support for explosion recipes in formulas

The system now automatically assigns explosion recipes to formula input items. A new Explosion Recipe column is available in the formula table to display these assignments. This column is hidden by default and can be added through personalization settings.

When the system explodes these recipes, the ingredient list is extended with items from the assigned explosion recipes. This provides a complete and accurate list of all ingredients and their percentages required to produce the primary output of the start recipe.

To enable ingredient calculations that include explosion results, administrators must configure a recipe calculation using the new Ingredients (With Explosion) calculation type.

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

To learn more about the feature, see Using Explosion Recipes.

 New personalization settings for Compare Recipes app

 Users now have more control over how comparison results are displayed in the Compare Recipes app.

  • You can now choose which columns to show in the comparison table by using the personalization settings.
  • A new Comparison Results column has been added. It shows both the comparison result definition and a visual indicator. The column is hidden by default.
  • You can also reorder columns in the comparison table by dragging them to the required position.

 

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

To learn more about the feature, see Comparing Recipes.

Enhanced access via OAuth2 client credentials for the Formulation APIs

 The OAuth2 Client Credentials flow has been expanded to include create and update operations alongside read operations, improving integration and data management workflows.

To learn more about the feature, see Using Formulation APIs.

 Specification Management 

Specification Management hybrid scenario ready with compositions 

The hybrid specification management scenario is now complete with users being able to load and transfer composition property values between specifications in SAP Integrated Product Development and their SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition system. This needs configuration and loading of such composition properties by your administrator, and also the implementation of SAP Note 3695812.

 [Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

To learn more about the feature, see Integrating with SAP S/4HANA Specification Management.

Ability to select and add specification versions to multiple composition items together

This feature allows you to select multiple specification version-based composition items at once (including a Select All option) when adding them to a specification's composition table.

To learn more about the feature, see 

CAD Adapters

SAP Integrated Product Development now supports direct integration with leading MCAD authoring tools. This marks a significant milestone for product-centric industries, closing a critical gap between engineering design environments and enterprise product data management.

To learn more about the feature, see CAD Interfaces Now Available in SAP Integrated Product Development.

More Information / Additional Links 

Also, check out our What’s New document for regular updates!

To learn more about the features of SAP Integrated Product Development, visit the SAP Supply Chain Management Enablement Hub.

You are invited to join the  PLM Experts Forum group on LinkedIn.

By the way, do you know how to provide feedback for SAP Integrated Product Development? Watch this video to find out how and send your feedback directly from the application. We always look forward to hearing from you.

entry.title: SAP Signavio Journey Modeler : A Practical Guide to Customer Journey Map and Journey Model
entry.content:
This blog outlines a well thought-out SAP Signavio Customer Journey Modeling functionality. A Journey Model illustrates an "Outside-In view" of the organization. This "Outside-In view" perspective can be of  a customer, Supplier, Partner, Applicant , or any other person interacting with the organization. For demonstration purpose, I have chosen the Customer Journey Map and Journey Model.

If you want to gain a deep understanding of SAP Signavio - Process Manager & Process Collaboration Hub, check out my blog post link below:

  • The nuts and bolts of a well-thought-out SAP Signavio : Process Manager & Process Collaboration Hub. 

SAP Signavio offers two ways to create Customer Journeys. One refers to design. The other refers to model a table-based Customer Journey.

  • Table-Based Customer Journey uses dedicated SAP Signavio Journey Modeler.
  • Design-Based Customer Journey uses the notation for Customer Journey Map.

Why Organizations need Journey Modeler?

Every customer Interacts differently with the organizations. Customer experience has a tremendous impact on organization`s business decisions. What it all boils down to is a "customer experience". If companies are in global competition, customer experience must be managed, so that it can be constantly improved. If customer gets a positive experience, it certainly enhances the organization`s Goodwill, and eventually increases organization`s sales and revenues.

What If customer gets bad experience, and organization turns blind eye?. What if organizations focus only on the smooth execution of their internal processes rather than looking at the things from customer point of view.

How can customer experiences be improved? 

Customer Journey Model(CJM) is the best way to improve customer experience. Customer Journey Model provides a new perspective. It allows organizations to see them from "Outside-In". Instead of focusing on internal operations, the focus shifts to the results of those operations and how the customer perceives the process. A journey model highlights the steps involved between the customer and the organization interactions from the customer perspective. These interactions happen as part of the journey of the customer experience to get a product or service.

My Business Use Case:

My Organization supplies Coffee Beans and Coffee Powder to coffee shop owners. I have leveraged SAP Signavio Journey Modeler to analyze and improve customer expectations across the end-to-end journey.

My Customers (Coffee Shop Owners) are as follows:

I will begin by explaining the customer Journey Map and then move on to Customer Journey Modeling(CJM).

When to Use a Journey Map and Journey Model:

Journey Map: Use customer Journey Map when

  • We want a high-level visualization of customer experiences.
  • We conduct brainstorming sessions.
  • We need executive-level storytelling.

Journey Model: Use customer Journey Model when

  • We need detailed Journey analysis.
  • We want to capture personas, touchpoints, sentiments, IT systems, and metrics.
  • We want to connect Journeys with business processes.
  • We are performing customer experience initiatives, process improvement.

Customer Journey Map:

The customer journey map is the visualization of the steps of customer interactions throughout customer journey with the organization, from discovery , to purchase , to post-purchase, and to re-order. A story based representation of how customer interacts with the organization. It focuses on:

  • Customer Actions
  • Emotions
  • Touchpoints
  • Experience
  • Pain points
  • Moment Of Truth

Customer Journey Map starts with the customer. Let us first create a persona.

Before we model customer journey map, we need to understand customers. Personas are widely adopted concept for breaking down quantitative customer data into customer archetypes that are easier to communicate and relate to. The characteristics of the personas define their customer journeys, each customer journey map should have exactly one persona. This persona should represent a relevant customer segment.

In SAP Signavio Process Modeler, we can add persona as a graphical element to a customer journey map and highlight key ideas, goals, and demands as style banners.

As part of this demonstration, since my customers belong to the same location, and they have had similar goals, expectations, and pain points, I have created two personas representing my customer segment. They run their own coffee shops, and buy coffee beans and coffee powder directly from my organization.

 

Mini Note: We can have current state customer journey map and future state customer journey map. In the future state journey map, we again identify the touch points with the business process landscape and compare with the ones identified in current state customer journey. From the result of the comparison, we can form the action items that become basis for the future state process landscape.

Map the "Current State"  Customer Journey

Now, as we know who our customers are and what they want , we start mapping out the customer journey. Each customer journey is a sequence of the core elements steps, touch points and moments of truth.

  • Steps are connected via simple lines and represents the sequence of events at high-level. At each step, the customers interact with the organization to have more product/service information, make decisions, or encounter a challenge.
  • Touchpoints represent steps where customer comes into direct contact, and each touch point relates to at least one of the business processes or IT systems.
  • Moment of Truth is the key decision point that can make or break organization`s business chance for succeeding with the customer. In essence, they are barriers requiring customer decision.

 

Let us choose the folder to place the Customer Journey Map.

Now, let us provide the title for Customer Journey Map as "Coffee Supply Customer Journey (AS-IS)" and start creating "Personas" and "Banners" to define our Customer Journey.

Let us connect the "Dictionary Objects" to elements of the Customer Journey Map.

Coffee Supply Customer Journey (AS-IS)

In my example scenario, the customer segment is irritated that my business doesn`t go the extra mile to make their path smooth to complete the end to end process. They have to follow up with sales rep for order confirmations , quality defects, and reorder request.

 

Let us now map the "Future State" customer journey.

Since we have known who our customers are, and what they want, we can start mapping out the customer journey. Each Customer Journey is a sequence of the core elements steps, touch points, and moments of truth.

Coffee Supply Customer Journey (To-Be)

 

Let us now publish Customer Journey Maps. 

 Post publish, we can mark the Customer Journey Maps as "Favorites" and view them on "Home" page.

 

Customer Journey Modeling(CJM): 

SAP Signavio Journey Modeler captures journeys in a tabular structure and connects what customers are thinking with the internal processes and analytics. In essence, it is a structured data-driven version of the Journey Map. It links processes and systems.

When we create a customer journey model, we depict an "outside-in view" of the organization.  While the business processes are best expressed in a diagram and mapped with BPMN, the experience of people on the receiving end of business processes is best expressed as a journey. With SAP Signavio Journey Modeler, we can capture customer journey in a tabular structure.

As part of this blog scenario, we explore various functionalities within the journey modeler:

  • Create a Persona within the dictionary
  • Create a Journey Model
  • Stages and Steps
  • Touchpoints & Text (Experience)
  • Sentiments
  • Link Business Process
  • Journey Complexity Score
  • Journey Model Dimensions

Persona within the Dictionary: 

Create a Journey Model:

 

Let us name the customer journey model as "Coffee Shop Owner Journey(Domestic).

let us select the folder for the customer journey model "Coffee Shop Owner Journey(Domestic)" to place in the Collaboration Hub.

Now, add the description for the Customer Journey Model "Coffee Shop Owner Journey(Domestic)" under the header area.

 

let us now link the Journey Owners/Personas/Journey Modelers

 

 

Define Stages and Steps:

The top row of the tabular journey model depicts the stages of a journey. The different stages represent points in the timeline. Each stage can contain multiple columns, commonly referred to as steps.

Now, let us add the stages and steps for our customer journey model.

 

Touchpoints & Text:

Touchpoints are points of contact between the customer and organization or product. We can use the option "Add Section" to add new sections. For Instance, let us add "Touch Points" and "Text" to update customer experience throughout the journey.

Text can be used to track customer experience at each step throughout the Customer Journey.

 

 

Sentiments:

This step is all about identifying the emotions of the customer. Each step triggers an emotion for the customer. The emotion can be positive, neutral, or negative. This emotion graph is used to measure the emotional level of the customer across the journey Customer Journey Model. This helps organization understand at which step customer is satisfied, or needs assistance, or is open to interaction. 

 

Link Business Processes:

We can link business processes diagrams for each step of the customer journey.

 

 

Metrics for Complexity Score: 

Complexity Score helps organization measure customer journey maturity. The perspective of someone interacting with the organization is expressed in a journey model. The complexity score gives an additional data point to evaluate customer journey models. It reflects the total operational complexity from linked BPMN processes and journeys.

With the complexity score, organizations can get an impression how challenging it is to complete the journey. The customer experience is improved by reducing the complexity of journeys and by making underlying processes more efficient.

We can use the following Metric Types for Customer Journey Model:

  • Net Promoter Score (NPS) : Customers holding positive, neutral, or negative feelings about the product or service.
  • Customer Effort Score (CES) : Customers rating  product or service on a scale from easy to difficult.
  • Customer Satisfaction Score (CSAT) : Customers rating their experience with the product or service on a scale from satisfied to dissatisfied.

We can add the metrics manually, or automatically with "Qualtrics" or "SAP Signavio API". For this scenario, I  have chosen manual option.

 

 

 

Journey Model Dimensions:

Journey Model Dimensions provide an at-a glance overview of the size of the journey model(grid size), and the number of nonempty fields. Detailed counts, for example, the number of linked personas and images are also provided.

Detail View panel shows detailed information in two parts. In the Structure Part, we can view the total number of stages, columns, and rows. The Content Part shows the different sections types in the Journey Model with their number.

For Linked Processes, each process is only counted once even the same process is linked to several cells. 

 

Now, let us preview and publish the customer journey model "Coffee Shop Owner Journey(Domestic)".

 

 

Once the Customer Journey Model is published, we can view the model under "Published" workspace view within the Collaboration Hub.

 

We can make use of the published Journey Model for future Journey Models when we save it as a template.

 

Let us enter the Template Name and activate the check box "Clear Content". The clear content option is to create a template with the same formatting of the current Journey Model.

Let us select a folder.

We must publish the template to create a new Journey Model from it.

Once it is published, we can view the newly published template under the Journey Model Templates tab.

 

Lessons Learned:

  • Customer pain points often become visible only when processes are viewed from customer`s perspective.
  • Departments may believe their internal processes are efficient, yet customers can still experience delays and frustration.
  • SAP Signavio Journey Models create a common language between business, process owners, and customer experience teams.
  • Sentiment, Moment Of Truth, and Touchpoints provide valuable context that traditional process models can not capture.
  • IT systems should be linked to journey stages to identify where technology improvements can positively impact customer experience.
  • Future-State Journey Modeling helps organizations prioritize transformation initiatives based on customer value rather than process convenience.

Conclusion:

SAP Signavio Customer Journey Modeling(CJM) provides the organization with the customer perspective on the business process landscape. By creating a Customer Journey Map and Model in SAP Signavio, organizations can connect Customer Journeys with internal business processes and have the basic foundation for optimizing customer experience.

😊This brings me to the end of process walk-through of SAP Signavio Customer Journey Map and Customer Journey Modeler. I hope this blog helps process teams understand high-level aspect of SAP Signavio Customer Journey Modeling (CJM) functionalities.

 

--

Yours Sincerely,

Pardhasaradhi Reddy. C

entry.title: Re: Regarding unexpected behavior in batch input for ME12
entry.content:
Here are my two cents on the matter:

  1. Ensure the add-on program fully refreshes or reloads vendor data before processing each record during mass updates.
    (Prevents stale data from affecting transactions.)

  2. Verify that no active sessions or unsaved changes exist in transaction ME12 before initiating mass maintenance.
    (Avoids conflicts with ongoing user activities.)

  3. Validate vendor master data for inconsistencies or special characters that may cause processing errors.
    (Ensures data integrity before execution.)

  4. Consider executing mass maintenance in separate batches per vendor group to isolate issues.
    (Improves error identification and minimizes disruption.)

entry.title: Re: SAP S/4 HANA Public Cloud: Inbound Delivery Printing...
entry.content:
Dear @Ley,

Based on SAP documentation there is no standard PDF output / print preview for the Inbound Delivery document itself in Public Cloud, unlike Sales Orders or Purchase Orders. The message No possible entries exist for this field when selecting an Output Type is therefore consistent with the standard limitation. SAP confirms that output for inbound delivery is currently not supported in the Cloud environment

More details below:

SAP KBA 3345360 - Output is not generated for Inbound Delivery

As a workaround, the output can be handled from the Goods Movement / Material Document side after the relevant goods movement is posted, not from the inbound delivery document itself. SAP KBA 3669809 explains that when goods movements are posted from areas such as Change Inbound Delivery / VL32N, output may not be generated automatically on the corresponding material document unless the relevant Output Parameter Determination settings are maintained for Goods Movement output for example GOODS_ISSUE_SLIP or GOODS_RECEIPT_PO_SLIP

More details below:

SAP KBA 3669809 - Goods receipt output missing from non inventory management apps

If the business requirement is a dedicated inbound delivery PDF form the correct approach is to submit an improvement request via SAP Customer Influence. SAP KBA 2963059 describes this as the channel for Public Cloud enhancement ideas, where customers can submit and vote for product improvements

More details below:

SAP KBA 2963059 - How to Submit Feature Requests for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: How to hide create invoice in J1IGSUBCON
entry.content:
There appears to be no available option to hide this functionality through authorization checks or via BAdI/Exit (no EXCLUDING option exists).

FORM set_pf_stat USING p_lv_stat.
  IF mod_cre = 'X'.
    SET PF-STATUS 'J_1IG_SUB'.
  ELSEIF mod_exp EQ 'X'.                                    "2600365
    SET PF-STATUS 'J_1IG_SUBEXP'.                           "2600365
  ELSE.
    SET PF-STATUS 'STANDARD'.
  ENDIF.
ENDFORM.                    " SET_PF_STAT

To address this requirement, you may adjust the GUI status via transaction SHD0.

entry.title: Re: 🐍 July Developer Challenge Week 1 — Model the Board
entry.content:
schema.cds

namespace snakeladder;

// ── Status enum — the heart of the state machine ──────────────────────────
type TurnStatus : String enum {
  Waiting;    // Session created, not yet started
  Playing;    // It's this player's turn — roll the dice!
  Moving;     // Dice rolled, player token is being moved
  Blocked;    // Player hit a double-headed snake — skips next turn
  ExtraTurn;  // Player rolled a six — gets another turn
  AFK;        // Player has been idle for too long — skips next turn
  Finished;   // This player has reached square 100 and won
}

// ── Board square — pre-loaded with snake/ladder data ──────────────────────
entity BoardSquares {
  key square      : Integer;        // 1–100
      snakeTo     : Integer;        // 0 if no snake on this square
      ladderTo    : Integer;        // 0 if no ladder on this square
      isDoubleHead: Boolean default false;  // true if this is a double-headed snake mouth
}

// ── Game session ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
entity GameSessions {
  key ID          : UUID;
      name        : String(100);    // e.g. "Friday Afternoon Game"
      createdAt   : Timestamp;
      startedAt   : Timestamp;
      finishedAt  : Timestamp;
      winner      : Association to Players;
}

// ── Player — one row per player per session ───────────────────────────────
@flow.status: TurnStatus
entity Players {
  key ID          : UUID;
      session     : Association to GameSessions;
      name        : String(100);
      @readonly
      position    : Integer default 1;      // current square (1–100)
      @readonly
      TurnStatus  : TurnStatus default #Waiting;
      turnOrder   : Integer;                // 1 = goes first
      lastRoll    : Integer;               // last dice result
}

game-service.cds

using { snakeladder } from '../db/schema'; 

service GameService {

  entity GameSessions as projection on snakeladder.GameSessions;

  @flow.status: TurnStatus
  entity Players as projection on snakeladder.Players actions {

    // Week 2 will implement these — declare them now so the model is complete
    @from: [ #Waiting ]
    @TO: #Playing
    action startTurn();

    @from: [ #Playing ]
    @TO: #Moving
    action rollDice() returns Integer;

    @from: [ #Moving ]
    @TO: #Waiting                  // transitions to next player in Week 3
    action confirmMove();

    @from: [ #Moving ]
    @TO: #Blocked                  // double-headed snake bite
    action blockPlayer();

    @from: [ #Blocked ]
    @TO: $flow.previous            // restores pre-block status
    action unblockPlayer();

    @from: [ #Moving ]
    @TO: #Finished
    action winGame();

    /*** Additional Actions ***/
    @from: [ #ExtraTurn ]
    @TO: #Moving
    action rollAgain() returns Integer;

    @from: [ #Waiting, #Playing ]
    @TO: #AFK
    action setPlayerInactive ();

    @from: [ #AFK ]
    @TO: #Waiting
    action IAmBack();
  }

  entity BoardSquares as projection on snakeladder.BoardSquares;
}

I extended the TurnStatus enum with ExtraTurn (for bonus rolls) and AFK to handle player inactivity in a multi-player setup.

entry.title: Re: Moving HTML5 Applications to Application Frontend Service
entry.content:
Hi Yashika

No, we are not sunsetting HTML5 repository, it will continue to be available and developed as needed. Application Frontend service brings new qualities for the HTML5 application development and operation that are not possible with HTML5 repository. While we aim Application Frontend more towards greenfield scenario, you can also easily move apps from HTML5 Repo into it, that what this blog is about. 
In any case, both services will reside side by side for the foreseeable future.

Regarding sync with SAP Build Work Zone, yes this is possible see here for more details. Currently this is a manual process, we are working to make it automatic.

I hope this helps.

entry.title: Re: 🐍 July Developer Challenge Week 1 — Model the Board
entry.content:
db/schema.cds

namespace snakeladder;

// ── Status enum — the heart of the state machine ──────────────────────────
type TurnStatus : String enum {
  Waiting;    // Session created, not yet started
  Playing;    // It's this player's turn — roll the dice!
  Moving;     // Dice rolled, player token is being moved
  Blocked;    // Player hit a double-headed snake — skips next turn
  Finished;   // This player has reached square 100 and won
}

// ── Board square — pre-loaded with snake/ladder data ──────────────────────
entity BoardSquares {
  key square      : Integer;        // 1–100
      snakeTo     : Integer;        // 0 if no snake on this square
      ladderTo    : Integer;        // 0 if no ladder on this square
      isDoubleHead: Boolean default false;  // true if this is a double-headed snake mouth
}

// ── Game session ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
entity GameSessions {
  key ID          : UUID;
      name        : String(100);    // e.g. "Friday Afternoon Game"
      createdAt   : Timestamp;
      startedAt   : Timestamp;
      finishedAt  : Timestamp;
      winner      : Association to Players;
}

// ── Player — one row per player per session ───────────────────────────────
@flow.status: TurnStatus
entity Players {
  key ID          : UUID;
      session     : Association to GameSessions;
      name        : String(100);
      @readonly
      position    : Integer default 1;      // current square (1–100)
      @readonly
      TurnStatus  : TurnStatus default #Waiting;
      turnOrder   : Integer;                // 1 = goes first
      lastRoll    : Integer;               // last dice result
}

srv/game-service.cds

using { snakeladder } from '../db/schema';

service GameService @(path: '/odata/v4/GameService') {

  entity GameSessions as projection on snakeladder.GameSessions;

  @flow.status: TurnStatus
  entity Players as projection on snakeladder.Players actions {

    // Week 2 will implement these — declare them now so the model is complete
    @from: [ #Waiting ]
    @TO: #Playing
    action startTurn();

    @from: [ #Playing ]
    @TO: #Moving
    action rollDice() returns Integer;

    @from: [ #Moving ]
    @TO: #Waiting                  // transitions to next player in Week 3
    action confirmMove();

    @from: [ #Moving ]
    @TO: #Blocked                  // double-headed snake bite
    action blockPlayer();

    @from: [ #Blocked ]
    @TO: $flow.previous            // restores pre-block status
    action unblockPlayer();

    @from: [ #Moving ]
    @TO: #Finished
    action winGame();
  }

  entity BoardSquares as projection on snakeladder.BoardSquares;
}

GameService endpoints screenshot:

I have used the same TurnStatus enum values to see how the builds in coming weeks, I can model the game at later stages by adding more values.
Additionally, I have used annotation path to get the response using Ajay's path

entry.title: Re: Issue During stock posting from purchase order
entry.content: If the stock type before clicking save icon shows the correct one (INSMK = X, quality inspection) and it only get to the wrong one after saving the document, a breakpoint can be set at statement CALL FUNCTION IN UPDATE TASK, to find out where the XEKPO-INSMK is updated.

entry.title: Re: Purchase order creation with a date that lies in the...
entry.content: The document date is proposed with the system date by default, but it can be manually changed before saving the document. To prevent the manual change to a future date, BAdI ME_PROCESS_PO_CUST can be used to add the custom check and issue a custom error.

entry.title: Re: Error in changes purchase order
entry.content:
Transaction SM13 can be used to view the details of the update termination error. It will show the function name where the error happens and the error message details.

To find out what data is imported and causes the error, you may set a breakpoint at the statement CALL FUNCTION IN UPDATE TASK and change the PO to reproduce the error. When you stop at the breakpoint which calls the function module, you can check the import data and verify which entry / field is wrong.
